title: The Tithe Bridge
special_element: **The bridge reads what each person values most, and will not let them pass until they set it down on the stones — and mean it.** Not forever. The item waits on the span, exactly where they left it, in plain sight the whole crossing; they may take it up again on the far side. But they have to let go of it for true, or the bridge knows. A warrior's father's sword. A merchant's last letter from someone dead. A child's luck-stone carried since before they could read. The bridge has no patience for decoys — it returns them, gently, to your feet.
summary: The bridge is narrow — one cart's width, no more — its stones worn smooth as old soap by ten thousand crossings.
body: The bridge is narrow — one cart's width, no more — its stones worn smooth as old soap by ten thousand crossings. The mortar between them is the colour of dried blood, and every parapet stone is carved with an open hand, palm up, waiting. Step onto the span and the whole structure *settles*: a low groan that runs abutment to abutment, stone talking to stone. Something has noticed you.
The Tithe Bridge has stood at this ford longer than the village behind you has had a name. Most travellers cross without incident. The bridge is not always hungry.

When it is, the carved hands are the tell. Locals call it *taking its due* — flat, matter-of-fact, the way they'd describe a tax. Outsiders call it a curse. The bridge does not care what it is called.

It does not want coin. The bandits camped at the near bank want coin — they hold the near bank and demand a toll of their own; the bridge is indifferent to theirs. The bridge wants something older. (This is a sacrifice-puzzle, not a haunting — for the bridge with a living thing beneath it, see *Under the Bridge*.)
Decide before play whether the bridge is live for this crossing. At easy difficulty, deploy it once — as a character moment, not a resource drain.
The reveal lands hardest when the *player* names what their character values — don't assign it, ask. *"What does [name] put down?"* Then give them a beat. The crossing is theirs, not yours.
If the party has already dealt with the bandits on the near bank, their refusal to cross becomes a clue they can now read: the boast about holding this ford rings false in a useful way.

Special element: **The bridge reads what each person values most, and will not let them pass until they set it down on the stones — and mean it.** Not forever. The item waits on the span, exactly where they left it, in plain sight the whole crossing; they may take it up again on the far side. But they have to let go of it for true, or the bridge knows. A warrior's father's sword. A merchant's last letter from someone dead. A child's luck-stone carried since before they could read. The bridge has no patience for decoys — it returns them, gently, to your feet.
trigger: The bridge is hungry only on a crossing the DM marks *live* — not every time. Signs it is live: the carved hands are warm to the touch, and the groan as you step onto the span runs longer than stone should make.
clues: The parapet carvings: open hands, palms up. Not threatening. Waiting.
Old offerings wedged in the stonework: a child's boot, a split arrowshaft, a plain ring with no inscription.
Any local, asked directly: *"It takes its due, when it's of a mind to. You'll know."*
The bandits at the near bank have not crossed in three weeks. They value their lives too much to mean it.
solution: Each character sets the object they value most on the stones, crosses, and takes it up on the far bank.
The test is sincerity, not loss. A character who genuinely values nothing they carry crosses freely — the bridge goes quiet under their feet.
consequences: **Comply:** the stones hold firm; the objects wait untouched, exactly where they were set.
**Refuse:** the stones shift — not enough to kill, enough to unsteady. The crossing is possible but lurching; fumble a check and something goes into the gorge anyway.
**Deceive:** the decoy comes back to your feet. The bridge waits. It has waited longer than you.
**Force it:** the bridge is old, and feels no pain, and has survived worse. Nothing happens to the bridge.

title: The Ledger Door
special_element: A true name opens the door — but the ledger keeps it. When a PC writes their name and the door swings, have them turn back, once they are through, and look at the page. Their name is already there, crossed out — in an older hand, in ink dry long before they stepped up to write. Somewhere in this ledger they were always here. And someone had already marked them as not coming back.
summary: Category: Puzzle · Tier: 1 · Difficulty: medium · Biome: ruins · Mood: mysterious
body: Category: Puzzle · Tier: 1 · Difficulty: medium · Biome: ruins · Mood: mysterious

The door has no handle, no keyhole, no hinge — pale stone set flush with the wall, seamless but for a brass bracket at chest height holding an open ledger. The pages are brown at the edges, the ink the colour of dried blood. The air is close and still, thick with old paper and, under it, something sharper: char, as though pages burned here an hour ago.

The ledger is the lock. Write a name and the door answers — but only to a true name. Aliases, titles, pet names, invented ones scorch off the moment the ink dries, leaving the paper clean. Only a name the writer truly answers to — a birth name, the name at the back of their own mind — holds. When it does, the door grinds inward on a breath of cold air.
The ledger is not blank. Every page within reach is full: names in a dozen hands across what looks like centuries. Most are legible. A few are struck through with one hard stroke.

If your table has met the self-updating roster at the Hollow Ward Post, the difference is the whole point: that ledger writes down who passes through, after the fact. This one was filled before anyone arrived. It does not record the living — it waits for them.

Special element: A true name opens the door — but the ledger keeps it. When a PC writes their name and the door swings, have them turn back, once they are through, and look at the page. Their name is already there, crossed out — in an older hand, in ink dry long before they stepped up to write. Somewhere in this ledger they were always here. And someone had already marked them as not coming back.
consequences: A character who reads old scripts can scan earlier pages: most are single names, no dates. A handful carry a margin mark — a circle with a line through it — and those names are also struck through.
The table beneath the bracket is scorched in repeated smears. Someone tried false names here, many times, and the charring runs deep.

A PC who studies the crossed-out names and beats a DC 12 Investigation or History check recognises one: a minor historical figure who vanished exploring these ruins. No record of their death was ever found.
The crossed-out name is the slow burn. It blocks nothing, but it is now a fact in the world. Who made the mark, and when? The answer may be sessions away.

Decide before play: does the circle-and-line carry mechanical weight — a curse, a pact, something that tracks the bearer — or is it simply a mark the door makes, its meaning dead with whoever built it? Either works; the second is harder to resolve and lingers longer.

title: The Drowning Stair
special_element: The ward is not listening to the words. It is listening to the pitch. Speak the right answer in the wrong register and the water still rises a step. Sing the *wrong* answer on the right note and the party earns a pause — one round in which the water holds and the ward's tone repeats, as if offering another chance. The puzzle is half riddle, half performance: knowing the answer is not enough. You have to be willing to sing it back.
summary: Category: Puzzle · Tier: 2 · Difficulty: hard · Biome: underwater · Mood: grim
body: Category: Puzzle · Tier: 2 · Difficulty: hard · Biome: underwater · Mood: grim

The stairwell drops in a straight column through black water, twelve steps just visible below the surface. Each one glows faintly — not with light but with the absence of silt, scrubbed clean by something old and patient. And from below, rising through the water and into your chest rather than your ears, comes a low repeating note. Not a sound. A pressure. A question, waiting to be asked.

The Drowning Stair links two submerged chambers across a vertical drop of roughly thirty feet. Whoever held this place sealed it not with a lock but with a voice-ward — a riddle sung into the stone at the building, set to play on repeat until it is answered.

The stair is half-dry at first. The bottom six steps are underwater; the top six are not. Every wrong answer — spoken, signed, or held up on a slate against the water — raises the level by one step. A right answer clears nothing on its own; the ward is not done with the party until it hears what it wants, the way it wants to hear it.

The riddle concerns carrying something without holding it: grief, knowledge, a name. The wording drifts each time it cycles; the logic does not. The answer is not unique — the ward accepts anything that fits.

When all twelve steps go under, the stair locks. The door at the column's base seals with a sound like an exhaled breath and will not reopen from this side. The water holds, still and permanent. The only way out is back up through the entry chamber — if anyone can still reach it.

**Before play, decide:** is there a strict correct answer, or does any sincere sung response on the right pitch count? The first rewards lateral thinking; the second rewards roleplay nerve, and plays better for groups who find riddles punishing.

**The ward's pitch** is a low chest note, near the bottom of a human singing range. Matching it is a DC 13 Charisma (Performance) check. On a natural 1 the attempt is far enough off that the water rises an extra step — the ward takes it for mockery.

**Reveal timing:** don't announce the singing mechanic. Let one or two wrong spoken answers raise the water first. When a player hums the answer, or sings it half as a joke, hold the water still a beat longer than it should be — and let them notice.

**If the party is drowning:** a previous visitor hangs in the water near the foot of the stair. They did not make it. A scrap of vellum is stiff in one hand. If retrieved, it shows the riddle, a possible answer, and one line crammed at the bottom in a hurried hand — *sing it.*
**At the table:** hum the note yourself. One sustained low note from the DM, held for two seconds before you describe the ward, changes the whole room. The players will not forget the session.

Special element: The ward is not listening to the words. It is listening to the pitch. Speak the right answer in the wrong register and the water still rises a step. Sing the *wrong* answer on the right note and the party earns a pause — one round in which the water holds and the ward's tone repeats, as if offering another chance. The puzzle is half riddle, half performance: knowing the answer is not enough. You have to be willing to sing it back.
